text: The manuscript describes a technique for robotic-assisted pancreatic
The represented patient is a forty-two-year-old female with a prior history of IPMN who presented with an acute onset of pancreatitis.
Diagnostic imaging including CT scan and endoscopic ultrasound revealed a complex septate of heterogeneous mass measuring three point three centimeters.
Biochemical workup was normal.
She was recommended for robotic RAPD.
The patient is positioned supine on a split leg table, with the right arm tucked and pressure points padded, preparing for the robotic-assisted pancreaticoduodenectomy.
The operating table is rotated 45 degrees to facilitate the positioning and use of the surgical robot during the procedure.
The XI robotic system is positioned and docked from the side of the operating table.
The robotic and assistant ports are visibly placed on the patient's abdomen, aligned according to a pre-determined configuration.
The surgeon places the liver retractor through the leftmost lateral port, positioning it to provide optimal exposure of the surgical field.
The liver retractor is positioned to retract the gallbladder and elevate the liver superiorly, maintaining this position throughout the resection phase.
The surgeon initiates the resection phase by entering the lesser sac, using surgical instruments to carefully access this anatomical space.
The surgeon accesses the lesser sac by dissecting through the greater omentum below the gastroepiploic pedicle, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The assistant applies gentle caudal counter-retraction on the exposed surgical field, aiding in maintaining optimal visibility and access during the procedure.
The surgeon continues the dissection along the greater curvature of the stomach, moving towards the pylorus, with visible tissue separation and manipulation.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the colon, separating it from the underlying duodenum, revealing the anatomical structures beneath.
The surgeon preserves the gastroepiploic pedicle, ensuring it remains intact without transection during the current phase of the procedure.
The surgeon performs Cokerization of the duodenum, a maneuver that involves mobilizing the duodenum by freeing it from surrounding tissues to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon grasps and transects the lateral fibers of the duodenum, separating them from surrounding structures.
The bedside assistant applies gentle medial counter-retraction to the duodenum, aiding in the exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon continues mobilization of the duodenum, focusing on its third and fourth portions, and extends the dissection towards the ligament on the right side.
The surgeon applies dynamic anterior and cranial retraction to the duodenum using an A-III retractor, enhancing the exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon completes extensive cokerization of the duodenum, providing full visualization of the inferior vena cava, the insertion of the left renal vein, and the aorta.
The surgeon completes the release of the ligament of Treitz, exposing the proximal jejunum, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon extracts the jejunum through the defect created in the ligament atrites, positioning it into the right superior colic upper quadrant.
The surgeon measures the jejunum approximately ten centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz, preparing for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device to ligate the mesenteric vessels, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a linear vascular stapler to transect the jejunum, visibly separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon performs linearization of the duodenum, sequentially ligating the mesenteric vessels of the proximal jejunum up to the uncinate process.
The surgeon opens the pars flaccida, carefully avoiding injury to the aberrant left hepatic artery visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon transects the stomach approximately five centimeters proximal to the pylorus, marking the designated transection point.
The surgeon ligates the right gastroepiploic vessels using a vessel sealer device, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
The surgeon transects the stomach using a thick linear stapler load, completing the separation at the predetermined transection point proximal to the pylorus.
The surgeon ligates the right gastric artery near its origin from the proper hepatic artery using 10mm laparoscopic titanium clips, followed by ligation with a blunt tip vessel sealing device.

The surgeon uses a grasper to hold the distal gastric staple line, retracting the specimen laterally and inferiorly, which places the common hepatic artery and the porta hepatis under visible tension.
The surgeon continues dissection along the superior border of the pancreas, carefully separating the pancreatic tissue from adjacent structures.
The surgical field extends into the porta hepatis, with visible tension on the common hepatic artery as the specimen is retracted laterally and inferiorly.
The surgeon identifies and resects the lymph node adjacent to the common hepatic artery, carefully excising it from the surrounding tissue.
The specimen is placed into a 10mm laparoscopic specimen retrieval bag and removed from the surgical field for permanent pathological analysis.
The surgeon achieves full visualization of the gastroduodenal artery, which is exposed due to the lateral and inferior retraction of the distal gastric staple line.
The surgeon identifies the gastroduodenal artery at its origin from the common hepatic artery, highlighting the anatomical relationship between these vascular structures.
The surgeon uses a robotic hook to circumferentially dissect the gastroduodenal artery, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
A vessel loop is passed around the gastroduodenal artery (GDA), encircling it for further manipulation or isolation.
The surgeon uses a vascular stapler to transect the gastroduodenal artery, completing the separation of this vessel from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon reinforces the proximal stump of the gastroduodenal artery using laparoscopic titanium vascular clips, each measuring ten millimeters, ensuring secure closure of the vessel.
The surgeon dissects the portal vein, extending the dissection two to three centimeters in a cephalad direction, revealing the anatomical structure and surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies and develops the plane between the common bile duct and the portal vein, carefully dissecting posteriorly to separate these structures.
The surgeon transects the common bile duct using a 60mm curved tip vascular linear stapler, positioned above the level of the visible biliary stent.
The surgeon uses robotic hook cautery to further dissect the lateral border of the portal vein, carefully separating it from adjacent tissues.
The surgeon encounters the superior pancreatic duodenal artery and ligates it using a blunt tip vessel sealing device, ensuring secure closure of the vessel.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the portal vein from superior to inferior, reaching the superior border of the pancreas.
The dissection reveals the superior tunnel, providing a clear view of the anatomical structures in this area.

The robotic arm A-III grasps the distal gastric staple line and pulls it laterally and cephalad, stretching the gastroepiploic vein as it enters the anterior surface of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV).
The surgeon uses electrocautery to open the fatty tissue near the inferior border of the pancreas, revealing underlying structures.
The superior mesenteric vein (SMV) is clearly visualized in the surgical field, indicating its exposure during the dissection process.
The assistant performs gentle dissection to create the inferior tunnel, working near the inferior border of the pancreas and adjacent to the portal vein.
The surgeon traces the right gastroepiploic vein to its insertion point at the superior mesenteric vein (SMV), highlighting the anatomical connection between these vascular structures.
The surgeon dissects the gastroepiploic vein, places a vessel loop around it, and ligates the vein using a sitzens blunt tip vessel sealing device.
The assistant retracts the specimen laterally at the distal gastric staple line, placing the pancreatic neck under tension for further dissection.
The surgeon transects the pancreatic parenchyma using monopolar curved scissors with electrocautery, visibly cutting through the tissue.
The surgeon carefully identifies the main pancreatic duct, ensuring precise visualization and preservation during the procedure.
The assistant lifts the pancreas anteriorly off the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) while the surgeon continues the transection of the pancreatic parenchyma.
The surgeon transects the main pancreatic duct using scissors, without the application of electrocautery, visibly separating the duct from surrounding tissue.
A four to five French pancreatic duct stent is placed into the main duct, ensuring clear identification of the duct within the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the transection of the remaining pancreatic parenchyma using electrocautery, visibly separating the tissue with controlled cauterization.
The surgeon performs slow and meticulous dissection of the pancreatic tissue, taking care to avoid significant hemorrhage during this critical portion of the resection.
The surgeon uses A-III to provide superior lateral retraction of the pancreatic head and uncinate process, facilitating dissection during this phase of the procedure.
The surgeon frequently assesses the placement of the A-III retractor, which dynamically aids in the dissection of the pancreatic head and uncinate process by providing superior lateral retraction.
The surgeon visually identifies the uncinate process, noting its composition of three distinct layers.
The surgeon identifies the first layer of the uncinate process, consisting of filamentous fibers located between the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and the pancreatic head and uncinate process.
The surgeon replaces A-one with a hook cautery device, using it primarily for dissection around the pancreatic head and uncinate process.
The surgeon exposes the second layer of the uncinate process, revealing the first jejunal vein, the vein of Belcher, and the uncinate branches.
The surgeon uses the A-one device and an assistant blunt tip vessel sealing device to perform dissection and ligation, focusing on the anatomical structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes the third layer, which is the SMA retroperitoneal margin, revealing its anatomical relationship with surrounding structures during the pancreaticoduodenectomy.
The assistant uses the A-two device to rotate the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and portal vein medially, revealing the anatomical structures surrounding the pancreatic head and uncinate process.
The assistant uses a blunt tip vessel sealing device to ligate vessels, ensuring secure closure within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a pancreaticojejunostomy using a two-layer end-to-side duct-to-mucosa anastomosis, employing a modified Blumgaard technique.
The assistant uses A-three to grasp the previously placed sutures, providing cranial retraction and enhancing exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon places two silk transpancreatic horizontal mattress sutures to secure the pancreatic parenchyma to the jejunum, ensuring stable attachment between these structures.
The surgeon places three sutures: one is positioned above, another below, and the third straddles the area, securing the pancreatic tissue.
The surgeon ties all three sutures, securing the pancreatic parenchyma to the jejunum, with the needles remaining attached to the sutures.
The surgeon carefully ties the middle suture that straddles the main pancreatic duct, ensuring that the duct is not accidentally ligated during the process.
The surgeon uses a pancreatic duct stent to assess the patency of the duct, ensuring it remains open and unobstructed.
The surgeon switches to monopolar scissors to perform the aneurotomy, carefully cutting through the tissue with precision.
This is then replaced to the needle driver.
The surgeon uses interrupted 5-0 PDS sutures to approximate the jejunal mucosa to the pancreatic duct, ensuring precise alignment between these structures.
The surgeon places a minimum of six sutures around the pancreatic duct, ensuring secure approximation of the jejunal mucosa to the duct.
The surgeon places additional sutures to accommodate the larger size of the pancreatic duct, ensuring secure approximation of the jejunal mucosa to the duct.
The surgeon inserts a pancreatic duct stent through the aneurotomy site before placing anterior interrupted 5-0 PDS sutures to approximate the jejunal mucosa to the pancreatic duct.
Once the anastomosis is complete,
The surgeon reutilizes three old silk sutures to secure the anterior layer of the pancreaticojejunostomy, following the placement of the posterior layers.
The surgeon performs a hepaticojejunostomy approximately ten centimeters distal to the previously completed pancreaticojejunostomy, connecting the hepatic duct to the jejunum.
The surgeon performs the anastomosis in a single layer, using either interrupted or running sutures to connect the tissues.
The surgeon uses monopolar curved scissors to transect the staple line of the common bile duct, visibly cutting through the tissue.
The surgeon uses bowel grasping forceps to approximate the jejunum to the common bile duct, preparing for an anastomosis.
The surgeon uses A-one with monopolar curved scissors to perform an aneurotomy, making precise incisions in the tissue.
The surgeon performs de-anastomosis using 5-0 PDA sutures in an interrupted fashion on ducts with a diameter less than one centimeter.
The surgeon uses two running 4-0 barb sutures to perform an anastomosis on larger ducts, applying the sutures in a single layer continuous fashion.
The surgeon places and ties the posterior sutures for an interrupted anastomosis, focusing on securing the tissue layers.
The surgeon inserts a four to five French biliary stent into a duct less than one centimeter in size to maintain the patency of the anastomosis.
The surgeon places additional 5-0 PDS sutures in the surgical field, continuing the interrupted suture technique for ducts with a diameter less than one centimeter.
The surgeon completes the anterior anastomosis, likely using sutures to secure the connection between the pancreatic duct and the digestive tract.
The surgeon ties the sutures after placement, completing the anastomosis with visible precision.
The surgeon performs a hand-sewn gastrojejunostomy, creating an anticholic, isoparastaltic anastomosis between the stomach and jejunum.
The surgeon places two 3-0 silk-marking stitches on the jejunum, approximately 40 to 60 centimeters distal to the hepatic jejunostomy.
The surgeon marks the proximal and distal sections of the jejunum, indicating the afferent and efferent limbs, using silk-marking stitches.
The surgeon replaces A-I and A-II with bowel grasping forceps, preparing to manipulate the bowel segments during the pancreaticoduodenectomy procedure.
The laparoscopic assistant reflects the omentum and mesocolon cephalad, providing a clear view for the surgeon to locate the neoduodenum.
The surgeon reduces the distal jejunum and positions it back into the infracolic compartment, completing the placement of the bowel segment.
The surgeon identifies the two marking stitches on the jejunum and brings the jejunum up to the stomach in an anticolic isoparastaltic manner.
The surgeon replaces the bowel grasping forceps with large dual-functioning needle drivers, preparing for further manipulation of the jejunal limbs.
The surgeon places an interrupted outer layer of sutures using two old silk sutures, securing the tissue layers in place.
The surgeon uses the most cephalic suture held by A-three as a retraction suture, providing tension and exposure to the surgical field.
The surgeon replaces the A-one suture with monopolar curved scissors, preparing for precise cutting or dissection in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses an instrument to transect the gastric staple line, visibly separating the stomach tissue along the pre-existing staple line.
The surgeon uses scissors to create an enterotomy in the jejunum, preparing the site for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon performs the anastomosis using two 3-0 barbed sutures in a running canal fashion, creating a continuous suture line between the two structures.
The surgeon places interrupted outer layer sutures using 2-0 silk to complete the gastrojejunostomy, securing the connection between the stomach and jejunum.
A 19 French round channel drain is positioned anterior to the pancreatic jejunostomy and hepatic jejunostomy following the completion of the anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a falciform ligament flap to cover the gastroduodenal artery (GDA) stump, ensuring protection and stability of the surgical site.
The surgical instruments are carefully removed from the operative field, and the robotic surgical system is undocked from the patient.
The surgeon closes the fascia and incisions in layers, using sutures to secure each layer of tissue.
The total operative time was two hundred twenty five minutes.
Estimated blood loss was twenty five mLs.
There was no post operative complications.
The final pathology revealed invasive moderately differentiated endocarcinoma arising from branch duct IPMN.
The manuscript provides the detailed steps of a robotic assisted pancreatic duodenectomy as performed at the University of Pittsburgh Medical Center.
